The reason $AAOI is such a volatile stock...

These dudes are either lying SO hard or this is the most asymmetric set up in the stock market.

Today they said they can go from $150M/quarter  to $470M/month in ONE YEAR.

Photonics TAM says yes. Mgmt track record does not. https://t.co/NCIQtroe9Q

Everything looks to be going to plan. Another situation with these photonics stocks where listening to management talk is way more important than the numbers. This is a forward growth story about becoming "a top 3 global laser supplier" with the ramp not starting until H2. The company raised full year guidance to $1.1B from $1B. Talked about their 900,00 sq. ft in U.S. factory space doing up to 30% production in USA on top of their 700K+ Taiwanese space Discussed their CPO laser which they plan to 80x production of (said they're in talks with several customers on this) Said margins could get to 40% towards end of year up from ~30% Working with 3 hyperscalers, one qualified, another almost qualified. Lin said real demand is $1.4-$1.5B even though they can only fulfill $1.1B. The company re-affirmed their target of hitting $450 monthly revenue in June/July 2027 which would imply an annual revenue run rate of $5B+ at a current market cap of ~$12B. This is what has triggered the massive re-rate. Execution risk remains crazy high but management just doubled down on what they previously said which is why the stock trade as high as $190 recently. I just don't think the strategically important infrastructure of AAOI (domestic laser fabs, vertical integration, CPO laser) is being given enough credit here. This could easily still be a multi-bagger if they execute, with the possibility of being worth several tens of billions in the next few years hence why it's the largest position in my photonics fund."}}}, "rest_id": "2052509803302555865", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "343377", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052528433226526947", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "97", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
COHR may be one of the most important AI infrastructure companies most investors still don’t fully understand.

Not GPUs. Not ASICs. Not memory.

Photonics.

Coherent sits at the physical layer of the AI buildout — the optical spine connecting GPUs, switches, and data centers.

Q3 FY26 revenue hit a record $1.81B, datacenter revenue grew 37% YoY, and NVIDIA just wrote a $2B check to secure the roadmap.

The bull case: 6-inch InP fabs, 800G → 1.6T → CPO, and a $23B growth SAM.

The bear case: debt, valuation, and execution risk.

Full deep dive below.

"to_index": 3582}, {"from_index": 3651, "richtext_types": ["Bold"], "to_index": 3685}]}, "text": "When you follow $SIVE closely over time, one thing that stands out is how many parallel tracks the company is advancing simultaneously. It rarely feels like a story built around a single product or market. Instead, it is a platform company where the same core photonics technology is applied across large and growing domains. One update that highlighted this breadth came on March 11, 2026, when $SIVE announced that a strategic LiDAR customer had chosen to integrate its photonics technology across all platforms. Production ramp is scheduled to begin in Q4 2026, targeting automotive and industrial applications. This is not a limited component agreement. $SIVE is supplying high-power continuous wave DFB laser diode arrays together with optical amplifiers integrated throughout the customer’s portfolio. Estimated cumulative lifetime revenue potential is $53–138 million, based on known volumes today, with clear upside as the customer scales. All indications point to this customer being $AEVA. Their 4D FMCW LiDAR technology, used in Atlas and X1, is engineered for long range, precise velocity measurement, and high resolution — capabilities required for real-world autonomous systems where safety and reliability are critical. $SIVE’s lasers help enable that performance, and their implementation across all platforms demonstrates the strength of $SIVE’s III-V photonics platform. It is worth stepping back and reflecting on what this means. The same technology stack powering highly efficient 1.6T transceivers with Jabil for AI data center infrastructure is now also finding its way into LiDAR sensors for trucks, passenger vehicles, robotics, and industrial automation. This ability to leverage one core competence across end-markets is one of the most compelling aspects of the $SIVE investment case. It lowers development risk, creates multiple growth engines, and allows ramps to mature at their own pace. $AEVA already has notable commercial momentum. They are integrated into $NVDA DRIVE Hyperion with volume production targeted for 2028 and have a partnership with Daimler Truck. As more OEMs, fleet operators, and robotics companies adopt similar LiDAR solutions, each new design win or deployment can drive higher shipment volumes. Automotive platforms often run for 8–10 years or longer, while industrial and robotics applications can create more stable long-term revenue streams. That gives $SIVE substantial room to grow beyond today’s base-case estimate. In the current market discussion, much focus is understandably on nearer-term opportunities — FWA and mmWave with Tachyon, or SATCOM progress through https://t.co/A7mlfHMHLp and $YSS. Those areas are important and may generate revenue momentum earlier. But the LiDAR ramp is a meaningful and underappreciated building block for 2027 and beyond. It does not begin as a small proof-of-concept. It starts with a strategic customer embedding $SIVE technology deeply from the beginning. This kind of multi-domain execution is what gives $SIVE its resilience and long-term potential. Photonics is the common thread connecting AI infrastructure (CPO), wireless connectivity (mmWave/FWA), satellite communications, and autonomous perception systems (LiDAR). With a reported pipeline exceeding $453 million and several parallel ramps in different stages of maturity, the company is not overly dependent on any single market or customer. The LiDAR opportunity still feels underappreciated. It may not dominate the daily discussion, but it starts contributing real production volumes already in Q4 this year. And as $AEVA expands in automotive and industrial markets, $SIVE’s participation across all platforms positions the company to capture a growing share of that opportunity. It is a reminder of why the longer-term thesis around $SIVE remains compelling: a specialized photonics capability that can scale across multiple high-growth verticals, creating revenue streams with significant long-term compounding potential."}}}, "rest_id": "2052353159398392187", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "525924", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052515063945199683", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "3437", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

PC에서 파워가 전력을 만들고, 메인보드·그래픽카드·RAM이 그 전력을 나눠 쓰듯이, 포토닉스 링크에서는 Laser가 만든 Optical Power가 Coupler → Waveguide → Modulator → Waveguide → PD로 이동한다. 문제는 이 과정에서 계속 Loss가 발생한다는 것이다. 보통 Laser Power는 mW가 아니라 dBm으로 본다. 쉽게 말하면, 100mW = 20 dBm 400mW = 26 dBm 이다. 400mW Laser는 100mW보다 출력은 4배 강하지만, dBm 기준으로는 +6 dB 높은 Laser다. 예를 들어 Laser 출력이 10 dBm이라고 해보자. Coupler에서 2 dB, Waveguide에서 2 dB, Modulator에서 3 dB 손실이 나면, PD에 도착하는 Optical Power는 결국 3 dBm 수준이다. 즉, 앞에서 아무리 강한 Laser를 넣어도 중간 Loss가 크면 PD가 받아들이는 Power는 크게 줄어든다. 반대로 말하면, Laser 자체가 무조건 강해야 좋은 시스템이 되는 것도 아니다. Laser에서 PD까지 가는 경로의 Loss가 낮으면, 더 낮은 Laser Power로도 충분히 쓸 수 있다. 그래서 포토닉스에서 중요한 질문은 단순히 “Laser가 얼마나 세냐?”가 아니다. 진짜 핵심은 이거다. 그 Laser Power를 PD까지 얼마나 덜 잃고 전달하느냐. 결국 Photonics는 Laser 싸움이면서 동시에 dB Loss 싸움이다."}}}, "quotedRefResult": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "rest_id": "2051926679506088437"}}, "rest_id": "2052017338346885248", "source": "Twitter for Android", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "48359", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2052513391898739130", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "777", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Anyone else grab $AAOI in the 130's?

Everything looks to be going to plan.

Another situation with these photonics stocks where listening to management talk is way more important than the numbers.

This is a forward growth story about becoming "a top 3 global laser supplier" with the ramp not starting until H2.

The company raised full year guidance to $1.1B from $1B.
Talked about their 900,00 sq. ft in U.S. factory space doing up to 30% production in USA on top of their 700K+ Taiwanese space
Discussed their CPO laser which they plan to 80x production of (said they're in talks with several customers on this)
Said margins could get to 40% towards end of year up from ~30%
Working with 3 hyperscalers, one qualified, another almost qualified.
Lin said real demand is $1.4-$1.5B even though they can only fulfill $1.1B.

The company re-affirmed their target of hitting $450 monthly revenue in June/July 2027 which would imply an annual revenue run rate of $5B+ at a current market cap of ~$12B. This is what has triggered the massive re-rate.

Execution risk remains crazy high but management just doubled down on what they previously said which is why the stock trade as high as $190 recently. 

I just don't think the strategically important infrastructure of AAOI (domestic laser fabs, vertical integration, CPO laser) is being given enough credit here. 

This could easily still be a multi-bagger if they execute, with the possibility of being worth several tens of billions in the next few years hence why it's the largest position in my photonics fund.

text:
If you invest in photonics, please read this.

I spent this weekend researching the OCS supply chain.

Quick lesson:

> OCS = Optical Circuit Switching
> Hyperscalers want lower power, lower latency, and faster AI networks
> OCS helps by keeping light as light
> Instead of optical → electrical → optical conversion at every hop, the signal can stay optical through the switch

That is why this is becoming a real theme.

$LITE already has a multi-year, multi-billi on-dollar OCS purchase agreement.

Management called OCS the “biggest single tightrope” the company is walking because customers want output faster than the supply chain can ramp.

$COHR doubled its OCS market opportunity estimate to over $4B.

They also said they fixed a production bottleneck and are now ramping output across two facilities.

$HUBN has Polatis, one of the more hidden OCS assets in public markets.

They secured major hyperscale OCS orders and opened a new Poland facility to increase capacity at least 5x over two years.

This is still early.

There is also an entire supply chain here.

So I built a public OCS map with 12 companies worth knowing.

I currently own 4 of them.

I want to own 2 more.

Read it here:

https://t.co/97bLrkIvE8

text:
$IPGP IPG Photonics: The Strategic Pivot, Path to Recovery, and Investment Thesis.

IPG Photonics is the dominant player in fiber lasers, but dominance in industrial cutting has become a more complicated position to hold. Chinese competitors have driven sustained price pressure in that segment, and the margin profile that once defined IPG's quality premium has eroded as a result. The strategic response — pivoting toward medical technology, battery welding, and defense — is the right directional move, but it's an early-stage reorientation against a backdrop of near-term financial pressure.

Recent results show demand recovering and revenue growing, which confirms that the core business remains viable. The more important question is whether that top-line recovery translates into margin restoration, and the answer so far is incomplete. Free cash flow conversion has been inconsistent, and profitability hasn't yet reflected the volume improvement in a way that validates the thesis.

Leadership has been overhauled. The new CEO is focused on operational discipline and broadening the product portfolio toward integrated laser systems — a higher-value configuration that moves IPG up the stack from component supplier to solutions provider. That's the right ambition, but integrated systems require different sales motions, longer customer relationships, and more complex delivery capabilities than standalone laser units. The organizational transition to support that shift is still underway.

The valuation is carrying a recovery premium that the financials haven't yet earned. The stock is pricing in a successful execution of the strategic pivot, which means margin expansion and free cash flow improvement need to materialize on a visible timeline to justify current levels. A high-quality business with a credible long-term direction, but the entry point demands that the complex pieces — new leadership, new markets, new product configurations — come together with reasonable speed.

The upside is real if the pivot lands. Medical, battery, and defense are structurally better markets than commodity industrial cutting, and IPG's technical foundation is strong enough to compete credibly in all three. The risk is execution timeline — the gap between strategic intent and financial proof points is still wide, and the stock doesn't offer much patience buffer at current multiples.

The strategic response — pivoting toward medical technology, battery welding, and defense — is the right directional move, but it's an early-stage reorientation against a backdrop of near-term financial pressure. Recent results show demand recovering and revenue growing, which confirms that the core business remains viable. The more important question is whether that top-line recovery translates into margin restoration, and the answer so far is incomplete. Free cash flow conversion has been inconsistent, and profitability hasn't yet reflected the volume improvement in a way that validates the thesis. Leadership has been overhauled. The new CEO is focused on operational discipline and broadening the product portfolio toward integrated laser systems — a higher-value configuration that moves IPG up the stack from component supplier to solutions provider. That's the right ambition, but integrated systems require different sales motions, longer customer relationships, and more complex delivery capabilities than standalone laser units. The organizational transition to support that shift is still underway. The valuation is carrying a recovery premium that the financials haven't yet earned. The stock is pricing in a successful execution of the strategic pivot, which means margin expansion and free cash flow improvement need to materialize on a visible timeline to justify current levels. A high-quality business with a credible long-term direction, but the entry point demands that the complex pieces — new leadership, new markets, new product configurations — come together with reasonable speed. The upside is real if the pivot lands. Medical, battery, and defense are structurally better markets than commodity industrial cutting, and IPG's technical foundation is strong enough to compete credibly in all three. The risk is execution timeline — the gap between strategic intent and financial proof points is still wide, and the stock doesn't offer much patience buffer at current multiples."}}}, "quoted_status_result": {"result": {"__typename": "Tweet", "article": {"article_results": {"result": {"cover_media": {"id": "QXBpTWVkaWE6DAAFCgABHH/dDWBW0AIKAAIZgoybDlbAAAAA", "media_id": "2053603004624850946", "media_info": {"__typename": "ApiImage", "color_info": {"palette": [{"percentage": 88.27, "rgb": {"blue": 254, "green": 254, "red": 254}}, {"percentage": 8.87, "rgb": {"blue": 98, "green": 50, "red": 26}}, {"percentage": 2.73, "rgb": {"blue": 157, "green": 128, "red": 116}}, {"percentage": 0.14, "rgb": {"blue": 172, "green": 147, "red": 135}}]}, "original_img_height": 793, "original_img_url": "https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HH_dDWBW0AIzX4q.jpg", "original_img_width": 1983}, "media_key": "3_2053603004624850946"}, "id": "QXJ0aWNsZUVudGl0eToyMDUzNjAyNjEwMTM4MjEwMzA0", "lifecycle_state": {"modified_at_secs": 1778454557}, "metadata": {"first_published_at_secs": 1778454557}, "preview_text": "EXECUTIVE OVERVIEW: IPG Photonics is no longer a simple high-power industrial cutting laser story. embedded_text:

https://t.co/CBoOOizljf

It is framed as these investors are driving this on speculation. https://t.co/nAA4yg4M3B

My take, SEK8B..what? Hence my reaction would probably be the opposite, this could be worth investigating: What thesis are these allocators actually trading? Is it pure speculation with SEK 8B in play? Who sits on these SEK8B? EFN however have asked "Richard Schatz, senior researcher in photonics at RISE Research Institutes of Sweden and former consultant for Sivers", for his opinion about Sivers. So what did Mr. Richard Schatz say: "There are several companies that manufacture these kinds of lasers, and Sivers is far from alone. I don't understand why the company has become so hyped. There are many players, not just in Europe and the US, but also in China," says Schatz. Honestly I have been COO and CEO at Sivers for almost 10 years 2015-2024 (2015 we had 18 headcount I known everyone in the Company until 2024) and also ask Board members that was in the board many years before me, both for Sivers and former CST Global (Sivers Photonics today) and none of them can recollect Mr. Schatz. I also sent an email, asking the reporter that interviewed Mr S and asked when he claims to have been a consultant at Sivers. Without reply. This doesn't really matter, it is just shows where the digging stops. Sivers Semiconductors is one of a handful of merchant InP laser suppliers globally. They have disclosed partnerships with POET Technologies, Jabil (1.6T LRO), O-Net, Ayar Labs and operate in the External Light Source category that scale-up CPO architectures depend on. Either are all these US investors dramatically wrong about the photonics layer of AI infrastructure, or the institutional allocators routing through US brokerages have identified what most Swedish investors haven't yet. Maybe this is worth investigating which? To be 100% clear no one knows (not even me), who has the right hypothesis, that we might know in 2028-2030 time frame. text:
This is something I didn't discuss back then, but it is essential knowledge

To understand $SIVE, we must recognize that two distinct branches exist:

-InP Lasers for Photonics (CPO Ramp up)

This is the segment currently catapulting the stock price

-MmWave Technology (Wireless)

In this branch, $SIVE possesses Radio Frequency (RF) technology capable of transmitting data over the air at ultra high speeds over short distances

Essentially, they manufacture chips and antennas for 5G, 6G, and Satellites (Satcom)

This is where the partnership with Tachyon Networks comes in developing 60GHz transceivers for $1.5M

Following a previous $3M deal for 28GHz transceivers

These technologies have reached a critical milestone

They are in the mass production phase with recurring orders

This is vital because while technologies like those from $ALMU or $KOPN are very promising, they are still far from this specific phase

The phase that often becomes a bottleneck and provides true vertical integration into the market

$SIVE’s wireless branch, however, is mature

While this second branch might seem less "flashy", it carries significant weight

Regarding the current revaluation of $SIVE, it is crucial as these figures are reflected in the earnings reports

Poor performance in this segment would tarnish the InP Laser branch, which is what the market cares about most right now

Furthermore, the fact that they are already operating at 28GHz and 60GHz proves that their silicon (InP) is reliable under real world conditions

Something that Photonics (CPO) customers examine under a magnifying glass before signing any contracts

"Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNTM1ODk2MzIwMjE5MzgxNzY=", "text": "This is something I didn't discuss back then, but it is essential knowledge To understand $SIVE, we must recognize that two distinct branches exist: -InP Lasers for Photonics (CPO Ramp up) This is the segment currently catapulting the stock price -MmWave Technology (Wireless) In this branch, $SIVE possesses Radio Frequency (RF) technology capable of transmitting data over the air at ultra high speeds over short distances Essentially, they manufacture chips and antennas for 5G, 6G, and Satellites (Satcom) This is where the partnership with Tachyon Networks comes in developing 60GHz transceivers for $1.5M Following a previous $3M deal for 28GHz transceivers These technologies have reached a critical milestone They are in the mass production phase with recurring orders This is vital because while technologies like those from $ALMU or $KOPN are very promising, they are still far from this specific phase The phase that often becomes a bottleneck and provides true vertical integration into the market $SIVE’s wireless branch, however, is mature While this second branch might seem less "flashy", it carries significant weight Regarding the current revaluation of $SIVE, it is crucial as these figures are reflected in the earnings reports Poor performance in this segment would tarnish the InP Laser branch, which is what the market cares about most right now Furthermore, the fact that they are already operating at 28GHz and 60GHz proves that their silicon (InP) is reliable under real world conditions Something that @UCF Thank you for holding the graduation ceremony last night, 5/9/26.  
I am so very proud of Joshua’s accomplishments on working hard to attain a Bachelor of Science degree in the field of Optics and Photonics.  He is a bright young man.  
Good luck in Delaware with the your next chapter of life.
I love you, Mom. 
#JoshuaLHendry

user_name: ゆるっと半導体｜AI・半導体ニュースを毎朝やさしく解説
text:
『Scintil Photonicsって、何してる会社？』 光集積回路（光信号を処理するチップ）の設計スタートアップなんだ。CEOのMatt Crowleyは物理学者出身で、研究室の技術を実際のものづくりに変える専門家。

2026年の今、Coherent・Lumentumといった光トランシーバー大手がCPO（共栄接光学）チップの増産に追われてるんだけど、こういう新興企業が『次の世代の回路設計』で割り込むパターンが増えてるんだ。

NVIDIAのBlackwell Ultra向け光学接続ケーブルの需要が膨らむなか、既存メーカーだけでは賄えない領域が出てきてるんだよ。Matt Crowleyのような『研究から量産への翻訳人』が活躍する土壌ができてる、ってのが今の市場の面白さなんだ。

→ AI時代の半導体投資では、有名大手より『こっそり成長する周辺企業』を先に見つけた人が勝つんだ。

text:
European and Asian Firms Express Strong Interest in Stakes in Canada’s Photonics Fabrication Centre Spin-Out 

The Government of Canada, through Industry Minister Joly and the National Research Council of Canada (NRC), is advancing the spin-out of the Canadian Photonics Fabrication Centre (CPFC)—North America’s only end-to-end pure-play compound semiconductor wafer fabrication facility—into a commercially oriented entity. The move, first signaled in Budget 2025 and formally announced in early May 2026, aims to attract private-sector capital to scale operations, expand Canada’s photonic semiconductor supply chain, and accelerate innovation in AI data centres, quantum computing, fibre-optic networks, and advanced technologies.

Key Context and Objectives

Strategic repositioning: Transition the publicly owned CPFC from a research-focused facility to a scaled commercial operation while maintaining majority Canadian ownership and control. The government will retain a stake, ensuring the entity remains anchored in Canada and continues to create high-skilled jobs.

Investment attraction: A formal call for proposals has been issued to secure private investment from “trusted partners.” The goal is to enhance domestic manufacturing capacity, support Canadian SMEs, and strengthen supply-chain resilience in critical photonics technologies.

Global interest: Minister Joly confirmed that firms from Europe and Asia have already expressed keen interest in taking stakes or investing in the facility. She highlighted opportunities to build a “middle-power alliance” by deepening ties with these partners, complementing North American demand.

Impact

This initiative positions Canada as a leader in photonic semiconductors—a foundational technology for AI, quantum, and high-performance computing—at a time of global supply-chain pressures. By leveraging international capital while safeguarding Canadian sovereignty, the spin-out is expected to drive economic growth, productivity, and technological sovereignty. Canadian companies such as Ranovus have already signaled intent to participate as investors or operators.

text:
Like many other FinX folks, I took a small position in $SIVE. This is one of my favorite speculative plays at the moment at ~3% of my portfolio, but….

I am keeping my eye on how well the 130 person org can juggle 5G mmWave + AI-DC photonics + LIDAR + SATCOM beamformers + defense + Nasdaq dual-listing prep + audit uplift. 

They have a lot of moving parts at the moment for a relatively small team ($LITE has 10k+ headcount for context). Perfect execution is imperative to maintain the 3B+ MC thesis. 

Right now, I still love this play at <50SEK

text:
$JEN $JNPKF the next SIVE with an existing solid business.

Jenoptik AG, a German technology company specialized in photonics, broke out today of its longterm (since 2018!) sideway range. The breakout followed the Earnings call today.

The core business includes Advanced Photonic Solutions, Smart Mobility Solutions, and Non-Photonic Portfolio. Recently Jenoptik transformed its business to a pure photonic player with the segments:

Nano-scale Micro-optics: Components for Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) lithography.
Silicon Photonics Testing: Solutions like the UFO Probe to validate optical chips.
High-Power Diode Lasers: Essential for medical and advanced industrial manufacturing.

To highlight is its more than expected surge of backlog. The total backlog has grown significantly to €719.2 million (up from €591 million at the end of 2025).

Jenoptik is a major supplier of ASML, Broadcom, Marvell and many more.

You'll find more information on my website including proposed entry and exit point as well as live signals when I buy and sell the stock.

text:
Big kudos to @pennycheck & @ThematicTrader for uncovering to me what genuinely feels like one of the most compelling AI infrastructure stories in the market right now👏🫶🐧 🐧 🐧 

$PENG sits right in the middle of the AI buildout across memory, CPUs, CXL, and even photonics. The Integrated Memory business alone is now running at roughly an $800M annualized pace growing 63% YoY, while the market still barely prices in the AI Factory platform, 85K+ deployed GPUs, or the asymmetric upside from their photonic memory architecture ambitions.

The more I dig into the story, the more it feels like Penguin is quietly positioning itself as a critical infrastructure layer for the inference era.

Really happy I managed to add more on today’s dip. I have a strong feeling this could grow into a massive company over the next few years

text:
$AMD  buying $6.5M of $MRVL at ~$99 wasn’t charity; it was supply‑chain alignment.

Hyperscaler AI buildouts favor silicon photonics and custom interconnects, which $MRVL sells.
Decision: ride $MRVL on dips while AI capex stays hot; trim spikes into earnings headlines.

text: @supaflyyy_ Lmao pls don't full port a stock. 3-5 names is a decent amount for a sub $200k portfolio though. Covering memory, adv packaging/glass, and photonics for now. Something like that.

The photonics industry is borne from chinese innovation that has happened at scale due to constraints including the need to overcome the semiconductor sanctions placed on it. It’s a geopolitical reality now. Quantum however is a pure scientific endeavor, the only way to model absolute reality.
embedded_text:
Photonics is the next quantum. 

Meaning it’s going to get decades of R&D and nothing will come from it.

text:
Every time I think about how AI can help with learning and understanding difficult concepts, I reflect on my days as a Master’s student. Some of the Optics and Photonics concepts I struggled so much to understand would have been far easier to grasp with today’s AI tools. Once in a while, I still prompt AI to break those concepts down, and now I understand them much better… how I wish this existed back then! 😅

Even this Monday, I was challenged to work with a very large dataset at work and present results to top management in less than 2 days. I started manually for about an hour and wasn’t even close to completing 2% of the task. I paused, opened my Jupyter Python notebook, described the problem to Copilot in simple language, and it generated a code template I could fine-tune. Boom — I completed the task in no time.

Honestly, these days I rarely see the need to deeply “study” data science the traditional way. With basic programming knowledge and the ability to think critically, AI can help you achieve almost anything. The game is changing very fast.

text:
Photonics is the future.
 
These are early days of the next leap forward.

Embrace it or don’t.

It’s coming either way.
embedded_text:
What an insane day for photonics.

$SIVE up 31.3%
$TSEM up 23.1%
$AAOI 20.01%.

It feels like a lot… but this just means you’re early to the next supercycle and there’s a lot of room to go.

Lot of people on X ask what’s next after $SNDK?

Here they are. https://t.co/jTeHpYtf0n

text:
"15 minutes of exposure to this can reduce blood glucose elevation by 27.7%."
— Journal of Biophotonics, 2024

"It activates the enzyme inside your cells that produces ATP, the energy currency of every living cell."
— Frontiers in Photonics, 2024

"It improves muscle recovery, accelerates wound healing, and supports tissue repair across dermatology, neurological function, and metabolic health."
— News Medical / Photobiomodulation review, 2026

All refer to red light.

The same wavelengths your great-grandparents got at sunrise and sunset every day.

The same wavelengths almost completely missing from modern indoor life.

My take, SEK8B..what? Hence my reaction would probably be the opposite, this could be worth investigating: What thesis are these allocators actually trading? Is it pure speculation with SEK 8B in play? Who sits on these SEK8B? EFN however have asked "Richard Schatz, senior researcher in photonics at RISE Research Institutes of Sweden and former consultant for Sivers", for his opinion about Sivers. So what did Mr. Richard Schatz say: "There are several companies that manufacture these kinds of lasers, and Sivers is far from alone. I don't understand why the company has become so hyped. There are many players, not just in Europe and the US, but also in China," says Schatz. Honestly I have been COO and CEO at Sivers for almost 10 years 2015-2024 (2015 we had 18 headcount I known everyone in the Company until 2024) and also ask Board members that was in the board many years before me, both for Sivers and former CST Global (Sivers Photonics today) and none of them can recollect Mr. Schatz. I also sent an email, asking the reporter that interviewed Mr S and asked when he claims to have been a consultant at Sivers. Without reply. This doesn't really matter, it is just shows where the digging stops. Sivers Semiconductors is one of a handful of merchant InP laser suppliers globally. They have disclosed partnerships with POET Technologies, Jabil (1.6T LRO), O-Net, Ayar Labs and operate in the External Light Source category that scale-up CPO architectures depend on. Either are all these US investors dramatically wrong about the photonics layer of AI infrastructure, or the institutional allocators routing through US brokerages have identified what most Swedish investors haven't yet. Maybe this is worth investigating which? To be 100% clear no one knows (not even me), who has the right hypothesis, that we might know in 2028-2030 time frame. To help anyone intressted, I made this free and open substack to explain how the money flows. As they said in Washington follow the money... text:
A great feature by MIT on Piris Lab, a Tekedia Capital portfolio company developing photonics systems to power the next-generation infrastructure of AI.

Largely, Piris Labs is building a full-stack AI inference platform designed to eliminate one of the most critical constraints in modern computing: the data movement bottleneck. By combining proprietary photonic hardware with a vertically integrated software stack, the company addresses the “memory wall” that limits the efficiency of today’s GPU-based systems.

Read here  https://t.co/0yykNVI8eL

Their approach delivers comparable performance to traditional compute clusters, at a significantly lower cost, by improving effective FLOP utilization and reducing latency. In doing so, Piris Labs is helping make the unit economics of trillion-parameter AI models truly sustainable.

Tekedia Capital >> we fund innovations

text:
The 800G to 1.6T transition is mid-ramp now, accelerating into 2026 alongside Rubin.

Test and validation aren't optional. Every new module needs production qualification.

If silicon photonics is the wave, $VIAV is the toll booth most AI portfolios are missing.

$VIAV: $12B cap, 8.8x sales, P/E 40.

Pure-play photonics names like $LITE and $COHR trade richer because the market prices them as direct beneficiaries.

VIAV gets paid by every transceiver vendor regardless of which one wins the 1.6T design slot.

Silicon photonics is the chokepoint Nvidia's Rubin platform is built around. Copper is out, light is in across every AI server.

Inside that basket, $VIAV sits at the test layer. Every 1.6T transceiver shipped gets validated on their gear before production. https://t.co/ExNUXvx0mr

text:
The technical setup for POET Technologies $POET  at $10.95 reflects a stock that is currently one of the most explosive "AI-Photonics" plays in the market. Following a multi-year basing period, POET has entered a high-conviction Strong Bullish Alignment, recently surging on massive institutional volume.

Here is the strategic breakdown of the "Optical Interposer" pivot and the technical setup for the week ahead.

📊 Technical Health Check: The "Breakaway" Gap
The system is flashing Bullish Alignment, with the price now trading in a "Power Trend" above both the Weekly Kumo and the Daily EMA.

Weekly Macro Trend: POET has successfully cleared the "Cloud of Death" that capped its price throughout 2024–2025. The Weekly Kijun-sen (purple) at $9.60 has officially flipped from resistance to a primary floor. As long as the stock stays above $9.50 on a weekly close, the macro bull case is the dominant narrative.
The Volume Profile: On May 8, POET surged 14.3% on over 70 million shares—more than triple its average daily volume. In technical analysis, this "Volume Spike + Price Breakout" is the ultimate confirmation that institutional accumulation is occurring.
The Signal Success: The Long Signal from April 20 at $8.59 is now up +27.5%. This trade was a "Macro Match" from day one, proving that the system correctly identified the shift in AI data center demand toward optical interconnects.

🧬 Fundamental Catalyst: 800G Scale-Up & Q1 Earnings

The $10.95 price point reflects the market's realization that POET is transitioning from a "research lab" to a "high-volume manufacturer."

Q1 Earnings (Thursday, May 14, 2026): POET is scheduled to report after the close this Thursday. While analysts expect a loss of $0.04/share, the market is solely focused on the Revenue Guidance and Backlog.

The $5M+ "Infinity" Order: Earlier this year, POET received a production order exceeding $5M for its 800G Infinity optical engines. Management has guided for delivery in H2 2026, and investors want to see if this has expanded.
The 1.6T Partnership (LITEON/Lessengers): POET is currently co-developing 1.6T and 3.2T optical modules. Any update on prototypes for these next-generation AI speeds (expected late 2026) would likely send the stock back toward its 52-week high of $15.50.
Manufacturing Readiness: High-volume production in Malaysia is slated to begin in Q2 (now). Any confirmation that the facility is "live" and shipping light-source products is the primary "Go/No-Go" signal for the bulls.

💡 Hybrid System Conclusion
The Bottom Line:  $POET is the "AI Bottleneck Buster." >The technicals are in "Strong Bullish" alignment because POET's technology addresses the #1 problem in AI data centers: Heat and Latency. 
The 70M volume day is a definitive market "vote" that this tech is real.
Trading Rule: With a massive 27% gain already in the bag, the risk of a "sell the news" event on Thursday is high. Do not chase the $11.00 level. Instead, look for a "Buy the Dip" opportunity if the stock pullbacks to the $9.50–$9.80 support zone (the Daily EMA). As long as it holds above $9.50, the trend is up.
Final Thought: We follow the photons, not the hype. POET has $430M in cash to bridge the gap to 2027 profitability.  Manufacturing scale-up is the current reality for $POET.

Summary: Resistance is at $12.30; Support is at $9.60. Thursday's call will determine if POET is a "Niche Component Maker" or the "Standard Interconnect" for the next generation of AI clusters.

Former CEO and board member of ANADIGICS, Trident Microsystems and Meru Networks, and later SVP at $GFS where he led the high-growth Mobile & Wireless Infrastructure business. CEO Vickram Vathulya previously led Nuvotronics through a strategic and operational transformation focused on long-term growth and value creation. Todd Thomson adds major financial and strategic firepower from Citigroup, GE Capital and Bain & Co. MD Wireless Harish Krishnaswamy brings world-class mmWave expertise and experience around strategic semiconductor funding opportunities like the CHIPS Act. On the photonics side, Andrew McKee brings deep technical and manufacturing expertise, while advisor Ian McCann has helped execute multi-billion-dollar semiconductor deals at Dialog, Linear and $ADI. CFO Heine Thorsgaard has spent 20+ years driving growth, operational efficiency and long-term value creation across technology companies, most recently as CFO of Napatech. And CRO Raymond Biagan has a strong track record of driving major revenue growth and customer expansion across the semiconductor industry, including at $GFS. Great technology alone doesn’t build billion-dollar companies. Execution does. text:
10. What is the market still missing?

In my opinion, the market is still looking too much at what $SIVE is today and not enough at what could happen if several ramps start layering in at the same time.

No one knows exactly how photonics, pluggables and CPO will develop, but I believe it will shock a lot of people how big it ultimately becomes. The market has a hard time grasping the scale before everything really kicks off. But once it does, the company could already be valued at multiples far beyond where we are today.

Jabil validation, WIN scaling, pipeline growth, institutional interest and Nasdaq path are lining up.

But if even a few of these ramps hit, I think the market may have to rethink what kind of company $SIVE is becoming.

$SIVE has been grinding for years. Now everything is starting to line up at once.

To me, this no longer looks like a small speculative semiconductor story. It looks like a company that may be entering the steep part of the curve just as several massive infrastructure markets begin accelerating at the same time.

And I still think the market is early.

What other questions around $SIVE should we dig into next?

text:
9. What are the biggest risks?

The risks are real: CPO adoption can take longer, volumes can slip, dilution exists, execution must be proven.

But the multi-market setup gives downside protection. If AI photonics takes longer, wireless, SATCOM, LiDAR and defense can still drive growth.

text:
6. Is this only an AI photonics story?

No, and that is exactly what many people miss.

AI photonics is the headline, but there are several other shots on goal. The same strong III-V platform and team is now showing up across AI, LiDAR, SATCOM, telecom and defense.

Personally, the F100 consumer electronics/biosensing angle is still the joker for me. I’ve been waiting on that one for years. If that lands properly, I genuinely believe it could take us to a 2.5B+ market cap overnight. Long-cycle stuff, yes, but qualification progress still seems to be moving forward.

text:
4. Why is the WIN Semiconductor partnership so important?

Because scaling photonics is not just about technology. It’s about manufacturing.

$SIVE chose an asset-light model. Glasgow for pilot production and control. WIN Semiconductor as the high-volume foundry path.

To me, WIN is the cost-down and scaling engine behind the entire photonics story. This is what separates the companies that can actually deliver at volume from those that stay small.

$AAOI 

According to the Management, target of... almost $500 MM in Revenue per month from mid-2027 on 😱🔥🔥

Clearly supply-demand situation in Optics/Photonics Networking is so ultra tight, the only question to get to that target is.... can they execute?

They confirmed that the prior $371/m projection for mid-2027 is REVENUE, not capacity 

AND

They raised that projection to $471/m for mid-2027 (not including CATV)

To put it in context, they just did ~$150m in QUARTERLY revenue

text:
Top 4 photonics + asymmetric 10 bagger setups for the next decade:

$SIVE - Sivers Semiconductors
Building the optical backbone for AI datacenters. Tiny market cap with exposure to co-packaged optics, laser arrays, and next-gen AI interconnects. One of the more underfollowed photonics plays in Europe.

$LWLG - Lightwave Logic
Electro-optic polymers that could drastically reduce power consumption and latency in AI networking. If polymers become part of future silicon photonics stacks, upside is massive.

$ALMU - Aeluma
Compound semiconductors + photonics on silicon. Positioned at the intersection of sensing, quantum, AI, and defense. Very early but huge optionality.

$WOLF - Wolfspeed
Silicon carbide infrastructure for the electrification and AI power era. High risk balance sheet, but if execution improves the rerating potential is enormous.

Wildcard asymmetric names:

$MRAM - Everspin Technologies
Persistent memory for AI, aerospace, industrial, and defense systems. Small niche today but strong long-term asymmetric potential.

$ALRIB - Riber
Advanced epitaxy systems critical for compound semiconductors, photonics, and future semiconductor infrastructure.

$LPK - LPKF Laser & Electronics
Precision laser manufacturing and advanced semiconductor packaging exposure. Strong positioning if photonics and advanced chip packaging accelerate.

$SVCO - Silvaco
EDA/software infrastructure play for semiconductor design, power electronics, and emerging photonics workflows. Underfollowed picks-and-shovels angle on the semiconductor cycle.

Given you can’t see atoms with the naked eye as they are usually sub angstorm and smaller then the the visible wave length of light, if may be a little while.

Firs use cases will be defect engineering, nano photonics where a small number of atoms placed intentionally can make all the difference in the world.

Then we build parts (see work of @mooreeth42 ).

This is simulated in AtomCAD https://t.co/EcXAunwiWG

Then we verify using various atomic scale imaging techniques (SPM/STM; TEM; AFM and Single
Atom NMRI (yet to be build)

Hope that helps.

And now Photonics Layer 1;

Layer 1 — Lasers and Light Sources
What this layer means: This is the most critical bottleneck in the entire silicon photonics stack. Silicon is an excellent medium for guiding photons, but it cannot generate them. Every silicon photonics system needs an external light source — almost always an InP-based laser. EML (Electro-Absorption Modulated Laser) and CW (Continuous Wave) lasers, high-speed photodiodes, Semiconductor Optical Amplifiers (SOA), and laser driver circuits all live in this layer. The supply crunch is real: NVIDIA has reportedly pre-allocated capacity at the world’s top EML suppliers through 2027, creating an industry-wide shortage that is reshaping the entire optical module market.

$LITE — Lumentum (also active in Layers 2, 5, 6) Currently the only supplier capable of shipping 200G-per-lane EMLs in volume production — the critical component for next-generation 1.6T pluggable transceivers. This single fact makes Lumentum arguably the most irreplaceable company in the near-term AI photonics supply chain. NVIDIA has locked in Lumentum’s EML capacity through a reported $2 billion multi-year commercial agreement. Lumentum also operates its own laser and PIC fabrication facilities (Layer 2), manufactures finished transceivers (Layer 5), and sells Optical Circuit Switch systems for data center networking (Layer 6). Active across four layers.

$COHR — Coherent (also active in Layers 2, 3, 4, 5, 6) The most vertically integrated company in the entire photonics stack. Coherent manufactures its own InP lasers (Layer 1), operates its own PIC foundry (Layer 2), assembles CPO modules (Layer 3), integrates DSP functionality (Layer 4), sells 800G and 1.6T pluggable transceivers (Layer 5), and provides coherent DCI transport systems (Layer 6). Fiscal 2025 revenue reached $5.8 billion, up 23% year-over-year. No other public company spans six layers of this chain.

$MTSI — MACOM Technology Solutions (also active in Layer 4) InP laser diode and high-speed photodiode manufacturer. MACOM’s proprietary Etched Facet Technology (EFT) enables wafer-level testing — a significant cost advantage over traditional InP production, which requires die-level cutting and testing. Also manufactures Trans-Impedance Amplifiers (TIA) and driver ICs, making MACOM active in both Layer 1 (lasers and photodiodes) and Layer 4 (signal processing components).

$AAOI — Applied Optoelectronics (also active in Layer 5) A vertically integrated laser and transceiver manufacturer based in the United States. AAOI designs its own lasers, builds them in its own fab, and ships them as finished transceiver products. Domestic U.S. manufacturing has become a strategic differentiator as supply chain sovereignty rises on the agenda of hyperscalers and government customers alike. Active in Layer 1 (laser manufacturing) and Layer 5 (transceiver sales).

$AVGO — Broadcom (also active in Layers 4 and 5) Present in Layer 1 as an EML supplier, but the center of gravity for Broadcom is in SiPh ASIC design (Layer 4) and silicon photonics transceivers (Layer 5). Broadcom’s approach integrates its own photonic chip design with laser components, producing a more unified product than competitors who source lasers externally. Active across three layers.

$AEVA — Aeva Technologies (new addition) Primary business is FMCW LiDAR, but Aeva has been moving its silicon photonics platform into data center applications. In January 2026, the company announced a high-power Semiconductor Optical Amplifier (SOA) delivering industry-leading performance for AI data center and Co-Packaged Optics architectures. The SOA addresses signal degradation in long-distance optical paths — a critical function in CPO systems. Aeva’s CoreVision module also integrates all LiDAR components onto a single SiPh chip, and this accumulated photonics expertise is being transferred toward data center use cases. Data center revenue exposure is still early-stage, but the SOA announcement is the entry point that puts AEVA in Layer 1.

$SIVE — Sivers Semiconductors Silicon-based laser and optoelectronic component developer. Working on laser integration using the Si/Ge platform rather than traditional InP — a potential cost advantage, but a technology that is still maturing toward production volumes.

$STM — STMicroelectronics (also active in Layer 4) Manufactures edge emitter lasers and optoelectronic components as part of a broad product portfolio. Also active in the mixed-signal and analog-to-digital converter segment, giving STM a presence in Layer 4 DSP support circuits as well.

$LASR — Laser component supplier (former II-VI ticker) Laser component and sub-assembly manufacturer. Now integrated under the Coherent umbrella following the merger, but still referenced separately in some industry analyses.

End of Layer 1. 

There are 6 layer more.  So you can read my full report in my substack as free; https://t.co/CZIKj5LfKX 

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

The Analytical Edge Strategy
May 11, 2026

A complete map of the silicon photonics supply chain — from raw indium to fiber in the ground

If you’ve been following AI infrastructure, you’ve probably heard that data centers are hitting what some call the Copper Wall — the point where electrical signals through copper can no longer keep up with the heat, latency, and bandwidth demands of training and running massive AI models. The answer is photonics: moving data as light through fiber instead of electrons through metal.

But photonics isn’t a single product. It’s a deep, layered supply chain stretching from exotic crystal growth in China to fiber trenching across American highways. This guide maps every layer of that chain, names every major public company in it, and explains exactly what each one does.

No data has been omitted. Every company from the original research is here.

Layer 0 — Raw Materials
What this layer means: This is the floor of the photonics supply chain. Before any chip can be designed, any laser built, or any fiber laid, the raw materials must exist. Indium phosphide (InP), silicon-on-insulator (SOI), gallium arsenide (GaAs), and optical-grade glass fiber are the fundamental outputs of this layer. Every laser, chip, and cable in the chain depends on what gets produced here. It is also the most geopolitically exposed layer in the entire stack: approximately 60–70% of global indium production comes from China.

$AXTI — AXT Inc. Single-crystal InP and GaAs wafer manufacturer. These substrates are the irreplaceable foundation for EML lasers and high-speed photodiodes. NVIDIA’s aggressive push toward 1.6T transceivers has turned InP into a supply bottleneck, and AXTI sits directly at that chokepoint.

$IQE III-V compound semiconductor epitaxy specialist. IQE grows thin-film layers of InP, GaAs, and GaN onto customer wafers, supplying the epitaxial substrate that laser diode and high-speed photodetector manufacturers need. Often called the invisible backbone of the optical module supply chain.

$SOI — Soitec Silicon-on-Insulator wafer manufacturer. SOI wafers are the starting material for silicon photonics foundries. TSMC’s COUPE platform, GlobalFoundries’ Fotonix process, and Tower Semiconductor’s SiPh line all rely on Soitec substrates. When a Broadcom or Marvell photonic chip ships inside a data center, there is a good chance the journey started on a Soitec wafer.

$WOLF — Wolfspeed Silicon carbide (SiC) and gallium nitride (GaN) wafer manufacturer. Not directly tied to photonic lasers, but critical for the high-power electronics and power converters inside data center infrastructure. Wolfspeed feeds the power layer that photonics depends on to function.

$GLW — Corning (also active in Layer 5) The world’s largest manufacturer of optical fiber and specialty glass. Every fiber cable running inside and between data centers starts here. Corning also produces specialty glass components used in transceiver housings and is a named partner in NVIDIA’s Spectrum-X silicon photonics ecosystem. Active in both Layer 0 (raw glass and fiber) and Layer 5 (module components).

$LWLG — Lightwave Logic Polymer-based electro-optic waveguide developer. Rather than silicon, LWLG uses organic polymer materials in the modulators that convert electrical signals to light — promising significantly higher modulation speeds and lower insertion loss. Still in the commercialization phase, making this an early-stage bet on a materials platform that could disrupt the conventional silicon approach.

$SHMN — Shin-Etsu Chemical (also active in Equipment layer) The world’s largest silicon wafer manufacturer. While SiPh foundries use SOI specifically, Shin-Etsu supplies the standard silicon wafer base that underpins the entire semiconductor ecosystem. Also present in the equipment layer as a foundry supply chain essential.

$VECO — Veeco Instruments (also active in Equipment layer) Manufacturer of MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) and MOCVD (Metal-Organic Chemical Vapor Deposition) equipment. Veeco does not make wafers — it makes the machines that grow them. III-V laser wafer production is impossible without MOCVD reactors, which is where Veeco’s role sits. Active in both Layer 0 (enabling raw material production) and the Equipment layer.

End of Layer 0.
There are 6 layer more.

So you can read my full report in my substack as free;
https://t.co/CZIKj5KHVp

My personal insights, not financial advice (NFA). Full disclosure in my pinned tweet.

text:
OCP is excited to announce our upcoming panel at Fiber Connect 2026's AI and Emerging Technology Infrastructure Summit on Wednesday, May 20!

Panel Title: Interconnect Insights on the Photonics Enabling AI Factory Scale and the Multi-Data Center Future

Join our expert panel for this discussion:
-Moderator: James Kelly, VP Market Intelligence & Innovation, OCP
-Sherwin Cabatic, President and CEO, NTT Devices America
-Jonmichael Hands, CEO, FarmGPU
-Stephen Manley, Sr Principal Network Engineer, Oracle Cloud
-David Hjerpe, National Account Manager, Databank

The race to exascale AI is fundamentally shifting how AI factory building blocks are designed and operated. This OCP-led panel will discuss how networking and photonics are becoming critical enablers for data movement, focusing on three key areas: scale-up within an AI pod, scale-out across the AI factory floor, and scale-across between AI data center campuses and metros. One practical reality we'll be discussing is that power density isn't the only headline-grabber: for data centers, fiber count and fiber pathway capacity per rack are so demanding that the cable plant is now essentially the new floor-loading, catching many colo designs flat-footed.

We look forward to seeing you at Fiber Connect!

Learn more and register here: https://t.co/J8WVlRM8fp

#FiberConnect2026 #AI #Photonics #DataCenter #OCP #OpenComputeProject #networking

Panel Title: Interconnect Insights on the Photonics Enabling AI Factory Scale and the Multi-Data Center Future Join our expert panel for this discussion: -Moderator: James Kelly, VP Market Intelligence & Innovation, OCP -Sherwin Cabatic, President and CEO, NTT Devices America -Jonmichael Hands, CEO, FarmGPU -Stephen Manley, Sr Principal Network Engineer, Oracle Cloud -David Hjerpe, National Account Manager, Databank The race to exascale AI is fundamentally shifting how AI factory building blocks are designed and operated. This OCP-led panel will discuss how networking and photonics are becoming critical enablers for data movement, focusing on three key areas: scale-up within an AI pod, scale-out across the AI factory floor, and scale-across between AI data center campuses and metros. One practical reality we'll be discussing is that power density isn't the only headline-grabber: for data centers, fiber count and fiber pathway capacity per rack are so demanding that the cable plant is now essentially the new floor-loading, catching many colo designs flat-footed. We look forward to seeing you at Fiber Connect! text:
What jumps out here isn't the $15B number itself, it's the shape of the spend. Nvidia isn't buying more chip designers or software shops. 

They're wiring themselves into everything that surrounds the GPU. Photonics, fiber, power, cooling, cloud capacity. That's a tell.

Bottleneck story has shifted. A year ago the conversation was about chip supply. Now it's about whether you can move data fast enough between racks, cool the room, and find the megawatts to plug it all in. Marvell and Lumentum solve the first problem. Corning and Coherent sit on the optical layer. IREN, CoreWeave, and Nebius are basically captive demand for Nbidia silicon dressed up as cloud companies.

Smart read for someone watching this space: Nvidia is hedging against its own success. If AI scaling stalls because of power or networking, their core business stalls too. So they're putting capital into the choke points before those choke points become someone else's pricing power.

Arista, Vertiv, Eaton speculation at the end is reasonable but already widely discussed. More interesting question is what happens to the second-tier names in each category when Nvidia picks favorites. Being un-picked in this ecosystem is starting to look like a real risk.

text:
Never ever FOMO. If you look hard enough, there are always new opportunities to be found.

Glass, photonics, CPUs, NeoClouds, power systems, memory, and everything semis that touches the sun will go up.

If the consensus now is that memory is simply safe to invest in and is the most commonly agreed-upon bottleneck, then the market will do the opposite in the short term, creating a new opportunity, most likely on macro bad news.

Money rewards those who are early and right (i.e. glass substrate) or patient and just on time (i.e. memory).

text:
Optical transceiver and silicon photonics packaging is the actual chokepoint in the AI infrastructure stack that the index trade misses.

Each AI cluster needs hundreds of thousands of high-bandwidth optical interconnects between racks, and packaging capacity is concentrated in a handful of Taiwanese names with multi-year qualified-vendor lead times.

The pricing power lives one step downstream of cell production where Foxconn-backed assemblers sit.

text:
$SIVE old news still very bullish 

Photonics AI interconnects target to $1.1 billion by 2028

Volume production expected to ramp by 2027 https://t.co/QDBFzmdrE6

The 10x Thesis: Identifying the Bottlenecks of the Next Decade $LWLG $SIVE $ALRIB $LPK $INFQ

This portfolio targets the "Picks and Shovels" of the fourth industrial revolution: AI infrastructure, Silicon Photonics, and next-gen Semiconductors. 

These companies aren't just market participants; they provide the critical hardware and software bridges that industry giants must cross to succeed.

1. $LWLG (Lightwave Logic)

The Bottleneck: Data centers are hitting a "speed and heat wall." Traditional silicon modulators are too slow and consume too much power for future AI needs.

The Edge: Their organic polymers enable data transmission at speeds exceeding 800G and 1.6T with significantly lower power. If this becomes the industry standard, LWLG replaces legacy photonics.

2. $SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors)

The Bottleneck: Massive AI clusters require seamless optical chip-to-chip communication, but laser sources are difficult to integrate.

The Edge: Their CW-WDM (Continuous Wave Wavelength Division Multiplexing) lasers are the "engine" for optical computing. 

3. $ALRIB (Riber SA)

The Bottleneck: You cannot build advanced compound semiconductors (like those for 5G or LiDAR) without atom-by-atom precision.

The Edge: Riber is the world leader in Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) machines. They are the gatekeepers; without their hardware, the manufacturers of specialized chips cannot exist.

4. $LPK (LPKF Laser &Electronics SE)

The Bottleneck: Organic chip substrates melt under the heat of high-end AI processors. The industry is pivoting to glass.

The Edge: Their LIDE (Laser Induced Deep Etching) technology is the only proven method to process glass-core substrates at scale. LPKF owns the key to the next generation of chip packaging.

5. $INFQ (Infleqtion)

The Bottleneck: Classical supercomputers cannot solve complex quantum physics or advanced encryption.

The Edge: Focusing on "Cold Atom" technology, they provide the hardware and software stack to make quantum computing commercial. They are building the infrastructure for a post-classical computing world.

6. $SPIR (Spire Global)

The Bottleneck: Real-time global data (weather, maritime, aviation) is often fragmented or unavailable in remote areas.

The Edge: Spire operates one of the largest Nano-Satellite constellations. They offer "Space-as-a-Service," providing high-margin, recurring data subscriptions for the global economy.

7. $WOLF (Wolfspeed)

The Bottleneck: Traditional silicon chips are inefficient for high-power applications like EV drivetrains and fast chargers.

The Edge: The global leader in Silicon Carbide (SiC). SiC chips increase EV range and reduce charging times. Wolfspeed owns the essential material for the global transition to electricity.

8. $OUST (Ouster)

The Bottleneck: For robots and autonomous vehicles to operate safely, they need to "see" in 3D with high resolutio, cameras alone aren't enough.

The Edge: By moving from analog to Digital LiDAR, Ouster has simplified the hardware into a single chip.
This makes their sensors cheaper, more reliable, and easier to integrate into "Physical AI."

9. $SVCO (Silvaco) 

The Bottleneck: Designing next-gen chips (Power, Photonics, Atomic-scale) requires specialized simulation software that standard tools can't provide.

The Edge: Silvaco provides TCAD (Technology Computer-Aided Design) software. They are a "bottleneck within a bottleneck", engineers at companies like $TSM or $NXP use it to design chips that drive the portfolio. 

10. $MRAM (Everspin Technologies)

The Bottleneck: Standard RAM is "volatile", it loses all data during a power failure, which is a disaster for industrial AI and data centers.

The Edge: They are the only commercial provider of MRAM (Magnetoresistive RAM). It is as fast as RAM but keeps data without power. It is the "forever memory" for critical AI infrastructure.

The 10x Goal: While these companies have smaller market caps, they hold the IP and market position required by giants like NVIDIA, Tesla, or Amazon. If even one of these "bottleneck" technologies becomes the global standard, the upside is exponential.

What's your favorite stock?

Not financial advice.

These companies aren't just market participants; they provide the critical hardware and software bridges that industry giants must cross to succeed. 1. $LWLG (Lightwave Logic) The Bottleneck: Data centers are hitting a "speed and heat wall." Traditional silicon modulators are too slow and consume too much power for future AI needs. The Edge: Their organic polymers enable data transmission at speeds exceeding 800G and 1.6T with significantly lower power. If this becomes the industry standard, LWLG replaces legacy photonics. 2. $SIVE (Sivers Semiconductors) The Bottleneck: Massive AI clusters require seamless optical chip-to-chip communication, but laser sources are difficult to integrate. The Edge: Their CW-WDM (Continuous Wave Wavelength Division Multiplexing) lasers are the "engine" for optical computing. 3. $ALRIB (Riber SA) The Bottleneck: You cannot build advanced compound semiconductors (like those for 5G or LiDAR) without atom-by-atom precision. The Edge: Riber is the world leader in Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) machines. They are the gatekeepers; without their hardware, the manufacturers of specialized chips cannot exist. 4. $LPK (LPKF Laser &Electronics SE) The Bottleneck: Organic chip substrates melt under the heat of high-end AI processors. The industry is pivoting to glass. The Edge: Their LIDE (Laser Induced Deep Etching) technology is the only proven method to process glass-core substrates at scale. LPKF owns the key to the next generation of chip packaging. 5. $INFQ (Infleqtion) The Bottleneck: Classical supercomputers cannot solve complex quantum physics or advanced encryption. The Edge: Focusing on "Cold Atom" technology, they provide the hardware and software stack to make quantum computing commercial. They are building the infrastructure for a post-classical computing world. 6. $SPIR (Spire Global) The Bottleneck: Real-time global data (weather, maritime, aviation) is often fragmented or unavailable in remote areas. The Edge: Spire operates one of the largest Nano-Satellite constellations. They offer "Space-as-a-Service," providing high-margin, recurring data subscriptions for the global economy. 7. $WOLF (Wolfspeed) The Bottleneck: Traditional silicon chips are inefficient for high-power applications like EV drivetrains and fast chargers. The Edge: The global leader in Silicon Carbide (SiC). SiC chips increase EV range and reduce charging times. Wolfspeed owns the essential material for the global transition to electricity. 8. $OUST (Ouster) The Bottleneck: For robots and autonomous vehicles to operate safely, they need to "see" in 3D with high resolutio, cameras alone aren't enough. The Edge: By moving from analog to Digital LiDAR, Ouster has simplified the hardware into a single chip. This makes their sensors cheaper, more reliable, and easier to integrate into "Physical AI." 9. $SVCO (Silvaco) The Bottleneck: Designing next-gen chips (Power, Photonics, Atomic-scale) requires specialized simulation software that standard tools can't provide. The Edge: Silvaco provides TCAD (Technology Computer-Aided Design) software. They are a "bottleneck within a bottleneck", engineers at companies like $TSM or $NXP use it to design chips that drive the portfolio. 10. $MRAM (Everspin Technologies) The Bottleneck: Standard RAM is "volatile", it loses all data during a power failure, which is a disaster for industrial AI and data centers. *AI infrastructure (including photonics) 
*IOT & Edge Computing
*Industrial Automation
*Sensors
*Medical Devices
*Defense & Aerospace
*Smartphones
*Electric Vehicles

As many countries seek to repatriate manufacturing back home in order to secure supply chain sovereignty and reduce dependency on Asia, $SILEX with its foundry production in Sweden is of strategic importance to Europe.

text:
Corning Investor day ; 

The "Springboard" Plan Extension & Upgraded Guidance
Corning has significantly raised and extended its long-term financial targets, driven primarily by an acceleration in its Enterprise Networks and new Photonics Market-Access Platforms (MAP).   
• 2026 Target Maintained: The company remains on track to hit a $20 billion annualized sales run rate by the end of 2026, representing a 15% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) from Q4 2023.  
• 2028 Target Upgraded: Management upgraded its internal plan to a $30 billion annualized run rate by 2028. This captures a $17 billion incremental "spring" in sales, a sharp increase from the $11 billion target they outlined just a few months ago in January. They established a "high-confidence" baseline target of $27 billion.  
• 2030 Target Initiated: Corning extended its timeline to 2030, targeting a $40 billion annualized run rate (with a high-confidence baseline of $35 billion). They project an accelerated 19% sales CAGR from Q4 2026 to Q4 2030. They specifically plan to build a $10 billion revenue stream solely from their new Photonics MAP by the end of the decade.  
The Catalyst: NVIDIA Partnership & AI Infrastructure
The massive upward revision in long-term guidance is fundamentally tied to the physical infrastructure demands of generative AI data centers.  
Alongside the guidance raise, Corning announced a multi-year, strategic partnership with NVIDIA. The deal focuses on co-packaged optics (CPO)—replacing traditional copper wiring with glass fiber in next-generation AI server racks (like NVIDIA's Vera Rubin) to drastically improve power and thermal efficiency.  
• The Investment: NVIDIA is making a $500 million upfront investment in Corning, with secured rights to invest up to a total of $3.2 billion.  
• Capacity Expansion: Corning is utilizing the capital to build three new manufacturing facilities in Texas and North Carolina. This buildout is expected to boost Corning’s optical connectivity manufacturing capacity by 10x and overall fiber production by more than 50%.  
Near-Term Earnings Context
It is worth noting that this long-term investor day update came shortly after a slightly mixed Q1 2026 earnings report in late April. While Corning posted a clean beat on Q1 revenue ($4.35 billion) and EPS, they issued Q2 revenue guidance of $4.6 billion, which slightly missed the consensus expectation of $4.65 billion. This caused short-term volatility before the May 6th long-term guidance upgrade sent shares surging again.  
Our View
The aggressive upward revisions for the 2028 and 2030 run rates highlight a material shift in the hyperscaler capital expenditure cycle. As AI clusters increase in scale-out density, the energy efficiency and token throughput requirements make the transition from copper to optical fiber unavoidable. By securing long-term, risk-shared capital agreements with key players like NVIDIA, Corning is effectively locking in its position as a critical, tier-one infrastructure supplier for the next wave of AI factory buildouts.

Gigaphoton⁠�

Dünyanın ən güclü fotonika şirkətlərindən biri.
Lazer sensorları, fotodetektorlar, elmi optika, tibbi və kvant texnologiyaları hazırlayır.

Yüksək dəqiqlikli motor və optik sistemlərdə istifadə olunan texnologiyalar hazırlayır.

Sənaye lazerləri, fiber lazerlər və fabrik avtomatlaşdırılması.
Panasonic Holdings⁠�
Optik və lazer əsaslı istehsal sistemləri üzərində işləyir.
Canon⁠� və Nikon⁠�
Litografiya və ultra dəqiq optik sistemlər.

text:
🚀 5/13 Copper Wall Account Update

Just restructured this account. Ditched the old $5/week into 12 AI/photonics stocks. 

New strategy: $65/week DCA for years into the key constraints & enablers of future compute demand. 

High conviction, speculative, but the upside if these theses play out is massive. Not devastating if they don’t.

Core Thesis: Tech keeps advancing. There will always be new constraints. Get ahead of them.

Thematic Exposure + Picks:

1.  Photonics / Lithography → $EUV (Corgi Semiconductor Photonics ETF)

2.  Memory (the AI bottleneck) → $DRAM (Roundhill Memory ETF)

3.  Space Infrastructure → $MARS (Roundhill Space & Tech ETF)

4.  AI Power & Infrastructure → $AIPO (Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF)

5.  Quantum Computing → $IONQ (direct stock)

This “Copper Wall” is built for long-term resilience in the next waves of tech. Who else is positioning for these themes?

Just shared with my investor group as we look at the big bottlenecks in memory, photonics, and energy...

And speaking of bottlenecks, our favorite engineer-teacher Vikram Sekar is using the Cerebras (CRBS) debut to highlight the 4 bottlenecks he thinks it will face and try to address.

Remember, this Cerebras "platform chip" is the size of home plate at your fav ball field. It's designed to do A LOT! (image below)

Only Vik could put together this deeper level of analysis on nanoscale bottlenecks as the AI Semi Ecosystem evolves rapidly in complexity, performance, and scale.
Here was his intro post on X...

text:
$HPE (Might be stupid) 

Seems to be overlooked as of now within the AI, memory, and photonics trade. HPE is an old dog that just made a new ATH and fits right into the next AI bottleneck. It's a secondary derivative to this trade. Phase 1 was chips and memory and Phase 2 is the system level bottleneck, which is how data is stored, and storage is becoming the next major issue.

HPE fits this build well and it is an established name with a solid book and customers including Nvidia and others. HPE built the enterprise grade object storage system designed specifically for this problem. The Alletra Storage MP X10000 is the first Nvidia certified object based storage platform for enterprise AI. On top of this, the Juniper acquisition puts them right into networking to reduce memory cost inflation.

If 30 can hold and theme stays hot why not see an old dog run vs the pre rev spec names ? Just a thought . Old yeller ?

text:
$ALMU Q3 2026 earnings: Narrative Outpaces Financial Reality as Guidance is Slashed

Aeluma's Q3 results present a stark contrast between an exciting AI photonics narrative and a sobering income statement. While management successfully secured $5 million in new R&D contracts, actual recognized revenue decelerated to $1.2 million, down slightly YoY. More alarmingly, government-related execution delays forced a massive cut to the top-end of FY26 revenue guidance. With operating expenses surging to support future manufacturing scale, Adjusted EBITDA losses have deepened. A strong $37.8 million cash pile buys the company time, but the math behind the narrowed guidance implies a brutal revenue cliff in the upcoming Q4.

Full article with charts - link in bio

🐂 𝐁𝐮𝐥𝐥 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐞
• 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐜𝐭 𝐏𝐢𝐩𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞 𝐒𝐞𝐜𝐮𝐫𝐞𝐝 — Aeluma met its FY26 target of 3-7 new engagements early, signing 6 new contracts worth $5 million. This non-dilutive funding validates the technology platform for government and defense applications.
• 𝐓𝐢𝐞𝐫-𝟏 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐮𝐟𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐏𝐚𝐫𝐭𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐡𝐢𝐩𝐬 — Announced critical partnerships with Tower Semiconductor and Sumitomo Chemical. This solves a major bottleneck for the company, clearing the path from R&D prototyping to volume production.

🐻 𝐁𝐞𝐚𝐫 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐞
• 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐈𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐞𝐝 𝐐𝟒 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐞 𝐂𝐨𝐥𝐥𝐚𝐩𝐬𝐞 — The company narrowed its FY26 revenue guidance to $4.2-$4.6M. Given that Aeluma has already generated $3.88M in the first nine months, this implies Q4 revenue will be just $0.32M to $0.72M—a massive deceleration.
• 𝐄𝐱𝐩𝐞𝐧𝐬𝐞 𝐄𝐬𝐜𝐚𝐥𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧 — Total operating expenses jumped 60% YoY to $3.3 million. The company is staffing up for commercialization, but with current revenues stalling, the cash burn rate is accelerating.

⚖️ 𝐕𝐞𝐫𝐝𝐢𝐜𝐭: 🔴
Bearish. The long-term AI data center thesis is compelling, but investors should not ignore the immediate financial math. A 23% cut to the guidance ceiling and an implied Q4 revenue drop outweigh the positive contract announcements.

𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐓𝐡𝐞𝐦𝐞𝐬

🔴🔴 𝐓𝐡𝐞 𝐐𝟒 𝐈𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐞𝐝 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐞 𝐂𝐥𝐢𝐟𝐟 [NEW]
The most critical data point in this report is buried in the math. Nine-month FY26 revenue sits at $3.88M. Management's new FY26 guidance is $4.2M-$4.6M. Subtracting the 9-month actuals leaves an implied Q4 revenue of just $0.32M to $0.72M. At the midpoint ($0.52M), this represents a 60% YoY collapse compared to Q4 FY25's $1.32M. This is a severe deceleration.

🔴 𝐎𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐞𝐱𝐩𝐨𝐬𝐮𝐫𝐞 𝐭𝐨 𝐆𝐨𝐯𝐞𝐫𝐧𝐦𝐞𝐧𝐭 𝐓𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬 [NEW]
Management directly blamed 'government shutdowns and other factors' for delaying the start-of-work on several projects, forcing the guidance cut. While the $5M in new contract wins is a strong positive, it highlights a structural vulnerability: Aeluma remains highly dependent on public sector bureaucratic timelines to recognize revenue.

⚪ 𝐀𝐜𝐜𝐞𝐥𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐡 𝐁𝐮𝐫𝐧 𝐭𝐨 𝐒𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭 𝐒𝐜𝐚𝐥𝐞
Operating expenses jumped from $2.1M a year ago to $3.3M this quarter. General & Administrative expenses alone rose 25% to $1.6M, while R&D nearly doubled to $0.88M. Management attributes this to key senior hires and operations scaling. This trend is accelerating, meaning profitability is moving further out of reach until commercial product revenue materializes.

🟢 𝐈𝐧𝐝𝐢𝐮𝐦 𝐏𝐡𝐨𝐬𝐩𝐡𝐢𝐝𝐞 𝐒𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐥𝐲 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐢𝐧 𝐆𝐚𝐩
The company's core thesis remains intact: the massive AI data center buildout requires advanced photonics, and the industry is constrained by Indium Phosphide availability. Customers are actively engaging Aeluma to bridge near-term supply gaps, validating the core technology platform.

🟢 𝐏𝐚𝐭𝐡 𝐭𝐨 𝐕𝐨𝐥𝐮𝐦𝐞 𝐌𝐚𝐧𝐮𝐟𝐚𝐜𝐭𝐮𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐂𝐥𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐞𝐝 [NEW]
Transitioning from R&D to commercial scale requires heavy-hitting foundry partners. The formal addition of Tower Semiconductor and Sumitomo Chemical Advanced Technology to Aeluma's supply chain is a major milestone. It proves to prospective commercial customers that Aeluma can deliver in volume.

𝐎𝐭𝐡𝐞𝐫 𝐊𝐏𝐈𝐬

𝐓𝐨𝐭𝐚𝐥 𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐡 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐄𝐪𝐮𝐢𝐯𝐚𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬: $37.8 million
Stable. Down slightly from $38.6M at the end of December 2025. Following their successful public offering earlier in the fiscal year, Aeluma has secured a massive liquidity runway. This cash buffer is the company's strongest asset, allowing them to absorb the current operating losses while waiting for commercial contracts to ramp.

𝐀𝐝𝐣𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐞𝐝 𝐄𝐁𝐈𝐓𝐃𝐀: -$0.91 million
Reversing. A year ago, the company posted a slight positive EBITDA of $109k. The recent quarters represent a structural reversal into deeper losses (-$917k in Q2, -$911k in Q3) as the company prioritizes team expansion and manufacturing readiness over near-term profitability.

𝐆𝐮𝐢𝐝𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞

𝐅𝐘𝟐𝟔 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐞: $4.2 - $4.6 million
Decelerating significantly. The company slashed the top end of its prior guidance ($4.0 - $6.0 million) by $1.4 million. Given the $3.88M already recognized year-to-date, this target implies an average Q4 midpoint revenue of just $0.52 million. Management attributes the shortfall to delayed project start dates caused by government shutdowns.

𝐊𝐞𝐲 𝐐𝐮𝐞𝐬𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬

𝐕𝐢𝐬𝐢𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲 𝐢𝐧𝐭𝐨 𝐅𝐘𝟐𝟕
With the start of several government contracts pushed out, how much of the $1.4 million removed from the top end of FY26 guidance will directly roll into Q1 or Q2 of FY27?

𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐦𝐞𝐫𝐜𝐢𝐚𝐥 𝐑𝐞𝐯𝐞𝐧𝐮𝐞 𝐓𝐢𝐦𝐞𝐥𝐢𝐧𝐞
The company highlighted $5 million in new development engagements. Are any of these engagements expected to transition from R&D to commercial product orders within the next 12 months?

𝐂𝐚𝐬𝐡 𝐁𝐮𝐫𝐧 𝐏𝐥𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐚𝐮
With operating expenses jumping to $3.3 million this quarter, have we reached a stabilized run-rate for OpEx, or should investors expect continued aggressive headcount expansion through the rest of the calendar year?
